data.title: Gold reclaims $5,000 and silver pops after the Supreme Court tariff ruling
data.description:
Gold and silver rallied sharply Friday afternoon as investors reacted to a significant legal development in Washington, driving renewed momentum across the precious metals complex.

Spot gold (XAUUSD:CUR) climbed 1.5% on the session to trade near $5,073 per ounce, pushing decisively back above the $5,000 threshold. Meanwhile, spot silver (XAGUSD:CUR) jumped 5.7% to $82.90 per ounce, marking its strongest level in roughly a week. 

The advance followed a closely watched 6-3 ruling from the U.S. Supreme Court that struck down former President Donald Trump’s tariffs. The decision invalidated the administration’s use of emergency powers to implement sweeping trade levies without explicit congressional authorization.

Market participants appeared to interpret the ruling as a catalyst for volatility across currency and macro-sensitive assets, prompting renewed demand for hard assets. Gold, often viewed as a hedge against policy uncertainty and geopolitical risk, attracted steady inflows.

Silver outperformed on a percentage basis amid heightened speculative interest, underscoring how quickly legal and policy developments can ripple through global commodity markets.

Gold ETFs: (GLD), (IAU), (SGOL), (OUNZ), (BAR), (GDX), (GDXJ), (NUGT), (RING), and (DUST). 

Silver ETFs: (SLV), (SIVR), (AGQ), (ZSL), (PSLV), (SIL), (SILJ), and (SLVP).

data.title: Southern Company wins two analyst upgrades after guidance 'clearly ahead' of expectations
data.description:
Southern Company (SO) won two analyst upgrades Friday, a day after the Atlanta utility reported Q4 results and raised its long-term earnings per share compound annual growth rate to 7%-8% from 5%-7% previously while raising its five-year spending plan by ~7%, reflecting the surging demand for power in the U.S.

The company, which said it had contracted 10 GW of large-load customers across Alabama, Georgia, and Mississippi, now plans to spend ~$81B through 2030, compared with its prior five-year plan of $76B, with about half of the spending directed to boosting power generation.

Data centers with a combined demand of ~75 GW have expressed interest in connecting to Southern's (SO) system, executives said on the company's earnings conference call.

Southern (SO) was upgraded at Mizuho to Outperform from Neutral with a $104 price target, raised from $89, saying the company still presents an attractive valuation for a fully regulated story "growing at 8% and possibly more," even after the stock's run-up.

"While investors will continue to focus on the political noise, we believe growth will drown out this noise and remind investors that the next GP rate case is in 2028," Mizuho's Anthony Crowdell wrote, adding that the shares have historically traded at one of the higher P/Es in the sector, "and with this quarterly update, we expect the march toward the mid-double-digit premium will begin."

Relative to other 8%-plus growers, the analyst likes that Southern (SO) is 90% fully regulated, does not depend on one customer, operates in a vertically integrated world, and has a Baa1 balance sheet.

The stock also was raised at Wells Fargo to Equal Weight from Underweight with a $96 PT, up from $84, saying updated guidance was "clearly ahead of our expectations, which came as a surprise and despite the affordability backdrop/optics" in Georgia.

Wells Fargo analyst Shahriar Pourreza said he has always believed in Southern's (SO) management team and core fundamentals, and his main concerns were centered around the political backdrop and affordability optics weighing on valuation, a risk that was "clearly overestimated."

Southern (SO) shares -0.7% on Friday after jumping 4.5% in the previous session following earnings.

data.title: Cogent anticipates 6%–8% multiyear revenue growth as on-net mix climbs to 61% amid Sprint runoff
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cogent Communications Holdings, Inc. (CCOI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO David Schaeffer highlighted a continued increase in margins, driven by cost reductions and a shift to more profitable on-net products. He noted, "Our on-net revenues as a percentage of revenues has increased from 47% of revenues in the third quarter of 2023 to 61% of revenues this quarter." Off-net revenues declined to 39%, and noncore revenues dropped to less than 1% of total revenues this quarter, reflecting a strategic focus on higher-margin offerings.
  • Wavelength services expanded to 1,096 locations, with revenue for the quarter at $12.1 million, marking a 74% year-over-year increase. Schaeffer stated, "Our wavelength revenue for the full year 2025...was $38.5 million, an increase of 100% from the 2024 number."
  • Schaeffer outlined the company’s deleveraging strategy, emphasizing improved leverage ratios and the intent to refinance $750 million in unsecured notes with secured notes once the make-whole period expires in June 2026.
  • Plans to monetize 24 surplus data centers were discussed, with negotiations ongoing after a previously discussed LOI was terminated due to owner financing requirements.
  • Schaeffer asserted, "We anticipate our revenue growth to continue to improve and be in the 6% to 8% range," with EBITDA margin expansion expected to moderate to roughly 200 basis points per year over a multiyear period.
  • CFO Thaddeus Weed confirmed, "Our total revenue for the quarter was $240.5 million and $975.8 million for the year. Our total revenue for the quarter declined sequentially by $1.4 million or by 0.6%. This was an improvement from the $4.3 million...sequential quarterly revenue decline that we experienced last quarter." He further broke down the revenue mix shift, noting a drop in Sprint-acquired wireline revenues and growth in Cogent classic revenues.

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed multiyear revenue growth targets of 6% to 8% and projected EBITDA margin expansion at a rate of approximately 200 basis points annually, after an exceptional 800 basis point improvement in 2025.
  • Schaeffer reiterated, "Our revenue and EBITDA guidance are meant to be multiyear goals and not intended to either be quarterly or even annual guidance."
  • No specific quarterly or annual guidance was issued, but management expects sequential revenue growth from this point forward as Sprint revenue runoff abates.

Financial Results

  • Total Q4 2025 revenue was $240.5 million, with full-year revenue at $975.8 million. Sequential quarterly revenue decline narrowed to $1.4 million compared to a larger drop in Q3.
  • On-net revenues (including wavelength) rose to $146.4 million for the quarter, up 7.8% year-over-year, while off-net revenues fell to $92.9 million.
  • Wavelength revenue for the quarter reached $12.1 million, a 19% sequential increase. Full-year wavelength revenue was $38.5 million, doubling year-over-year.
  • Gross margin for the quarter improved to $112.5 million, up 100 basis points sequentially to 46.8%. Full-year gross margin rose to $442.7 million, a 720 basis point improvement from 2024.
  • EBITDA (excluding IP Transit payments) for the quarter rose to $51.7 million, with margins up to 21.5%. Full-year EBITDA (classic) was $192.8 million, a $70 million increase over 2024.
  • Capital expenditures in the last half of 2025 were $73.3 million, reflecting the completion of data center modernization. Full-year CapEx was $187.6 million.
  • Leverage ratios improved slightly, with gross debt leverage at 7.35% (vs. 7.45% in Q3) and net debt ratio at 6.64% (vs. 6.65%).

Q&A

  • Christopher Schoell, UBS: Asked about revenue and EBITDA outlook as Sprint revenue runoff diminishes and waves business scales. Schaeffer responded, "We will be back to positive revenue growth on a quarterly basis from this point forward, and we anticipate that the annual rate of growth on average over a multiyear period will be in that 6% to 8% range."
  • Sam, for Greg Williams, TD Cowen: Sought clarity on the waves funnel target and data center LOI. Schaeffer explained, "Our funnel is continuing to grow, but we will not be reporting specific numbers," and detailed the shift to backup negotiations for data center sales.
  • Sebastiano Petti, JPMorgan: Asked about installed but not billed waves and data center sale progress. Schaeffer indicated installed-but-not-billed base was comparable to last quarter and that proceeds from data center sales are not assumed in refinancing plans.
  • Frank Louthan, Raymond James: Inquired about IPv4 leasing and data center market value. Schaeffer noted, "Today, we're about 46% of our addresses are leased...we do still have half of our address space that is sitting fallow," and expects continued growth in leasing.
  • Brandon Nispel, KeyBanc: Probed on Sprint vs. Cogent revenue split analysis. Schaeffer admitted more rapid than expected Sprint revenue decline and described the manual process undertaken to disaggregate revenues.
  • Additional questions covered data center sales, refinancing specifics, cost savings, sales rep productivity, and leverage targets, with management consistently reiterating a focus on high-margin growth and deleveraging.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on clarity around revenue trajectory, the pace of Sprint runoff, data center monetization, and debt refinancing. Questions frequently sought more granular details and forward-looking metrics.
  • Management’s tone was confident in the prepared remarks, with Schaeffer stating, "We are enthusiastic and optimistic about our wavelength business," and "We are absolutely committed to not materially changing our return of capital...until we reach 4x net leverage." During Q&A, the tone remained measured, with occasional hesitation, as in, "I'm not sure if we can repeat that, but we will continue to see further growth," regarding IPv4 leasing.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, there was a shift to more constructive dialogue on sequential revenue growth and less defensiveness about Sprint runoff. Analysts appeared cautiously optimistic, while management increased transparency on revenue mix and deleveraging progress.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Sequential revenue decline narrowed to $1.4 million in Q4 from $4.3 million in Q3. On-net revenue growth continued, while off-net and noncore revenues further contracted.
  • Gross margin and EBITDA margins improved more than in the previous quarter, reflecting cost reductions and a higher on-net revenue mix.
  • Management’s sentiment shifted from addressing headwinds and capital allocation changes in Q3 to emphasizing margin expansion and renewed growth in Q4. Analyst focus moved from concern over revenue declines and dividend cuts to questions about the sustainability of growth and the pace of deleveraging.
  • The company provided greater detail on the breakdown between Sprint-acquired and Cogent classic revenues, responding to investor interest.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified continued deterioration in the acquired Sprint revenue base, with Schaeffer stating, "We are still experiencing significant monthly and quarterly sequential degradation in that business."
  • Challenges persist in monetizing surplus data centers after the prior LOI failed due to financing terms. Negotiations with multiple parties are ongoing but outcomes remain uncertain.
  • There is exposure to integration costs, with incremental expenses still ongoing but expected to end by year-end.
  • Leverage remains elevated, though improving, with management prioritizing a reduction to 4x net leverage before resuming aggressive capital returns. Refinancing risk is present ahead of the June 2027 note maturity.

Final Takeaway

Cogent Communications enters 2026 with a higher-margin, more on-net focused revenue base following the integration of Sprint and acceleration in wavelength and IPv4 leasing businesses. Management reaffirms a multiyear target of 6% to 8% revenue growth and ongoing margin expansion, while actively pursuing surplus asset monetization and debt refinancing. The company signals confidence in its ability to drive profitable growth, further reduce leverage, and eventually return capital to shareholders as the impact from Sprint revenue runoff abates and high-margin products drive future performance.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Forum Energy Technologies outlines 6% revenue growth and $55M–$75M free cash flow target for 2026 while advancing market share gains
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Forum Energy Technologies (FET) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Neal Lux, President, CEO & Director, highlighted that "fourth quarter and full year results once again display why FET is a great business and a compelling long-term investment." He emphasized disciplined execution amid lower global drilling activity, tariffs, and geopolitical uncertainty, noting market share gains driven by the "Beat the Market" strategy and a 20% increase in revenue per global rig since 2022.
  • Lux stated that in 2025, despite a sizable decline in global rig count, FET increased revenue per rig and achieved a full year book-to-bill of 113%, with subsea product line nearly reaching 190% book-to-bill and international drilling product orders rising.
  • The company enters 2026 with its highest year-end backlog in 11 years, up 46% since the start of 2025. Lux attributed this to innovation, citing ten new products commercialized in 2025, including the SecuraSlim stage collar and DURACOIL 95.
  • Structural cost reductions and plant consolidations are set to deliver approximately $15 million in ongoing annualized savings. Free cash flow generation was cited as a defining strength, with $80 million delivered in 2025, enabling a 28% net debt reduction and repurchase of approximately 11% of shares outstanding.
  • Looking ahead, Lux projected 2026 revenue of $800 million to $880 million, EBITDA of $90 million to $110 million, adjusted net income between $18 million and $38 million, and free cash flow between $55 million and $75 million. Lux stated, "This is a great start to executing FET 2030."
  • David Williams, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "Fourth quarter revenue of $202 million exceeded the top end of our guidance range and increased 3% sequentially." He noted strength in offshore and international markets, with international revenue surpassing U.S. revenue for the second consecutive quarter. Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter reached $23 million.

Outlook

  • Management is guiding 2026 revenue between $800 million and $880 million, EBITDA of $90 million to $110 million, adjusted net income of $18 million to $38 million, and free cash flow between $55 million and $75 million. Lux projected, "We are forecasting revenue growth of 6% and EBITDA to increase by 16%."
  • Williams provided Q1 2026 guidance of $190 million to $210 million in revenue, EBITDA of $21 million to $25 million, and adjusted net income between $5 million and $9 million. He added, "We expect to generate positive free cash flow this quarter."
  • The company expects to convert 65% of EBITDA into free cash flow in 2026.

Financial Results

  • Quarterly revenue reached $202 million, with offshore and international segments showing 7% and 8% sequential growth, respectively, while U.S. revenue declined 2%.
  • Adjusted EBITDA was $23 million for the quarter, with higher revenue and cost reductions offsetting less favorable product mix and increased expenses.
  • Fourth quarter book-to-bill was 93%, reflecting order timing in the Drilling and Completions segment. Segment revenue breakdown: Drilling and Completion $127 million (up 8%), subsea product line up 25%, coiled tubing up 13%, and drilling products up 11%.
  • Artificial Lift and Downhole segment revenue was $75 million (down 4% sequentially). Free cash flow for the quarter totaled $22 million, with full year free cash flow at $80 million.
  • The company ended the year with net debt of $107 million and liquidity of $108 million. Two real estate sale-leaseback transactions generated $15 million in net cash proceeds.

Q&A

  • Jeffrey Robertson, Water Tower Research LLC, asked about subsea business trajectory and product benefits from global unconventional development. Lux responded, "2026 will be a year where we're going to convert a lot of our backlog and look to add on for '27 and beyond," and noted opportunities in Argentina and Saudi Arabia for new technology adoption.
  • Robertson asked about acquisitions. Lux replied, "Another downhole type business would be interesting...the main criteria though is, is it a great business? Are we adding something...that has differentiated solutions...accretive to FET without stressing the balance sheet."
  • Steve Ferazani, Sidoti & Company, LLC, asked about Q4 performance drivers. Lux noted, "We just didn't have that end of the year slowdown around Christmas. We call it frac holiday in years past, and we didn't see that." Williams added, "Good revenue growth in the subsea product line... a little bit of maybe ahead of the game on subsea projects."
  • Ferazani questioned Q1 guidance strength amid declining rig count. Lux said, "It's a continuation...of our Beat the Market strategy. We are gaining share and expanding on that."
  • Ferazani asked about free cash flow guidance. Williams explained, "Walking from EBITDA down $35 million of cash taxes and interest, then $10 million of CapEx, so really in line with what we've done...and a $10 million release or source of cash from working capital."
  • Ferazani inquired about share buyback timing. Williams stated, "We did repurchase about 400,000 shares...in the fourth quarter...I think buybacks may be a little more back-end loaded this year."
  • Keith Beckmann, Pickering Energy Partners LP, asked about growth avenues in Drilling & Completions and Artificial Lift. Lux highlighted subsea and international artificial lift opportunities, aiming to double share in growth markets.
  • Beckmann followed up on order margins and lead times. Lux detailed that 75% of revenue is activity-based consumables with quick turnaround, and capital equipment has about a 6-month lead time. Incremental EBITDA margin goal is 30%.
  • John Daniel, Daniel Energy Partners, LLC, focused on M&A opportunities and seller valuation expectations. Williams indicated, "We've seen some lift in seller expectations...as public company stock multiples increase."
  • Eric Carlson raised questions spanning U.S. market torque, non-oil and gas markets, acquisition multiples, and capital allocation. Lux and Williams discussed U.S. rebound potential, defense and data center market opportunities, discipline in M&A, and the balance between buybacks and acquisitions.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a positive to slightly positive tone, congratulating management on performance and probing into the sustainability of growth, capital returns, and M&A strategy. Questions were direct but largely supportive, with no overt skepticism detected.
  • Management’s tone was confident and forward-looking during prepared remarks, with Lux asserting, "We remain confident in our bullish long-term outlook." Responses in the Q&A were measured, providing detail on operational execution, cost control, and free cash flow, while emphasizing ongoing discipline and strategic flexibility.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the sentiment remains confident with a continued focus on execution and long-term strategy. Management's confidence appears consistent, with slightly more emphasis on strategic growth and capital allocation flexibility.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is more explicit with revenue, EBITDA, and free cash flow ranges, building on 2025 results and raising the bar for capital returns and backlog conversion.
  • The backlog at year-end 2025 is the highest in 11 years, up 46% from the previous year, compared to the prior quarter’s reference to a 21% quarterly backlog increase.
  • Strategic focus has shifted toward international expansion, product innovation, and margin discipline, with management citing structural cost reductions and a streamlined plant footprint.
  • Analysts’ focus evolved from structural cost initiatives and backlog conversion to detailed inquiries about product-specific growth, margin profile, and capital allocation.
  • Management’s tone remains confident and disciplined, reiterating the importance of the Beat the Market strategy and FET 2030 plan.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted persistent challenges from tariffs, global drilling softness, and geopolitical uncertainty, but cited pricing actions, supply chain optimization, and manufacturing flexibility as mitigation strategies.
  • Analyst concerns centered on sustaining working capital efficiency as the company enters a growth phase, margin realization on new orders, and execution of capital returns amid leverage constraints.
  • Tax complexity and exposure to international tax rates were discussed, with Williams emphasizing ongoing compliance and optimization efforts.

Final Takeaway

Forum Energy Technologies enters 2026 with record backlog and clear strategic direction, targeting 6% revenue growth and $55 million to $75 million in free cash flow. Management highlights ongoing market share gains, disciplined cost controls, and robust product innovation as drivers for continued EBITDA improvement and capital returns, while maintaining flexibility to pursue accretive acquisitions or share repurchases as market opportunities arise.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Western Union outlines 6%–9% 2026 revenue growth with Intermex integration and digital expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Western Union Company (WU) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Devin McGranahan, President, CEO & Director, emphasized the ongoing transformation into a digital-first, retail-enabled consumer services company powered by payments and innovation. He stated, "We remain optimistic about the longer-term outlook for our business as we believe our core retail remittance business will improve as migration patterns normalize, and we work to increase both our revenue and share gains in this important market." McGranahan highlighted stabilization in the Americas retail segment and improvement in certain corridors, with "U.S. to Mexico quarter improved hundreds of basis points relative to the third quarter." He also noted the successful launch and early traction of the V-Go Money wallet in the U.S., as well as wallet expansion plans for Australia, Mexico, Singapore, the Philippines, and Israel.

  • McGranahan announced several exclusive partnership wins, including the return of Deutsche Post, a five-year exclusive contract with Canada Post, and a long-term deal with Vallarta Markets. He stated, "With these partnerships...we expect at least an incremental $100 million of retail revenue per year when fully ramped."

  • Matthew Cagwin, Executive VP & CFO, reported, "For the full year, we delivered GAAP revenue of $4.1 billion." He added, "Adjusted EPS was $0.45 in the fourth quarter compared to $0.40 a year ago." Cagwin also discussed progress in the Consumer Services segment, which contributed 14% to total revenue in Q4 with 26% adjusted revenue growth, and outlined the impact of ongoing cost discipline.

Outlook

  • Cagwin provided a 2026 guidance for adjusted revenue growth of 6% to 9%, inclusive of Intermex, with an adjusted EPS outlook for the full year between $1.75 to $1.85. He stated, "Our adjusted revenue outlook for 2026 is for 6% to 9% revenue growth, inclusive of Intermex, which we now expect to close in the second quarter of this year."

  • He noted the expectation for Consumer Services to have another strong year in 2026, with the Travel Money business "expected to approach $150 million in revenue."

  • Management highlighted that EPS is expected to accelerate as the year progresses due to seasonality in Travel Money and timing of expense benefits, specifically relating to operational efficiency programs and Intermex integration.

Financial Results

  • McGranahan reported Q4 revenue of $1 billion, which represented a 5% year-over-year decline on an adjusted basis. Consumer money transfer transactions were down 2.5% in the quarter, but cross-border principal growth was up on a constant currency basis.

  • The branded digital business increased transactions by 13% and adjusted revenue by 6% in Q4, with gains aided by new relationships in the Middle East. Consumer Services adjusted revenue was up 26% in Q4 and nearly 30% for the full year, driven by Travel Money and bill payments.

  • Cagwin highlighted an adjusted operating margin of 20% for the full year, up from 19% in the prior period. Operating cash flow for 2025 was $544 million, including $220 million in cash taxes paid related to the transition tax.

Q&A

  • Tien-Tsin Huang, JPMorgan: Asked about the outlook in light of January and February trends and Intermex contribution. Cagwin responded that "we are seeing an improvement relative to our Q4 performance 6 weeks into the year" and factored Intermex into Q2 close estimates, stating results are "largely consistent with what Euronet published."

  • Huang followed up on retail agent wins and exclusivity costs. McGranahan explained these are "exclusive deals, which is part of the strength of the Western Union brand," and Cagwin added the deals "will contribute to our overall OI."

  • Darrin Peller, Wolfe Research: Inquired about sustaining digital transaction growth and the spread between transaction and revenue growth. McGranahan identified Middle East partnerships and digital platform improvements as growth drivers, while Cagwin attributed the widening spread to lower revenue per transaction in new account-to-account partnerships.

  • William Nance, Goldman Sachs: Asked about digital revenue growth assumptions and Intermex revenue in the guide. Cagwin stated the guide includes a "modest improvement" in digital growth and Intermex is factored as a Q2 close with growth rates aligned to North America trends.

  • Rayna Kumar, Oppenheimer: Questioned Intermex accretion and stablecoin demand. Cagwin reiterated the $0.10 per share accretion target for the first full year post-close, and McGranahan noted no strong sender demand for stablecoin, but opportunities for on- and off-ramping digital assets.

  • Timothy Chiodo, UBS: Asked about flattening customer acquisition trends. McGranahan cited increased competitive offers and evolving digital marketing landscapes as factors, with strategy shifts underway.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, focusing on clarity of digital growth drivers, the impact of new partnerships, and the implications of the Intermex acquisition. Questions often pressed for details on competitive positioning and margin profiles.

  • Management maintained a confident and proactive tone during prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing operational resilience, new partnerships, and digital initiatives. McGranahan stated, "We are excited about the opportunities in front of us."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remains probing yet constructive, while management tone is slightly more confident given recent wins and stabilization in key areas.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 shows an increase over previous outlooks, with 6% to 9% adjusted revenue growth including Intermex, versus reaffirmed 2025 guidance in the prior quarter.

  • The current call featured stronger emphasis on exclusive agent partnerships and expansion of the digital wallet strategy, including new market launches.

  • Analysts in both quarters focused on digital growth and retail stabilization. This quarter, questions shifted more toward margin impacts of exclusivity deals and integration plans for Intermex.

  • Management’s tone has become more assertive, reflecting confidence in cost discipline, digital expansion, and partnership wins. Analyst sentiment remains inquisitive but less skeptical than the prior quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • McGranahan cited ongoing macro volatility, geopolitical events impacting migration, and the implementation of the U.S. remittance tax as key challenges. He noted, "through the first 6 weeks of this year, we have not seen a material impact on our business, but we continue to monitor the situation closely."

  • Cagwin cautioned that Consumer Services growth may be affected by market conditions and integration of new partners, but remains optimistic due to historical performance.

  • Analysts raised concerns about digital acquisition flattening, competitive pressure on pricing, and volatility in key corridors like U.S. to Mexico.

Final Takeaway

Western Union management projects confidence in its multiproduct transformation, underpinned by digital growth, exclusive agent partnerships, and the pending integration of Intermex. While macro and regulatory headwinds persist, the company expects stabilization in key retail corridors, ongoing margin strength through cost discipline, and incremental revenue from new agent wins. The digital wallet strategy and new product offerings are poised to support growth, with management targeting 6% to 9% adjusted revenue growth for 2026 and a continued focus on operational efficiency and shareholder returns.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: The software split: Road builders vs. toll booths
data.description:
Software stocks have become a crucial battleground for investors lately, as the market struggles to evaluate the potential impact AI will have on the industry. SA analyst Jack Bowman has discussed a framework for evaluating players in this industry, distinguishing between “road builders” and "tollbooths."

According to Bowman, this bifurcation helps explain why companies like Salesforce (CRM) and Adobe (ADBE) are being sold off while MongoDB (MDB) and Palantir (PLTR) maintain strength.

"Road builders are these companies that produce dashboards. They’re just made for humans to use as a tool," the analyst explained on a recent episode of Seeking Alpha's Investing Experts Podcast. "And then you’ve got tollbooths, the companies that provide databases and tools that are bot-callable for AI to actually use in their workflow. These are not replaceable because the AI actually needs them."

The concern that AI could replace knowledge workers affects road builder companies directly, Bowman argued. However, tollbooth companies benefit regardless of whether humans or AI agents are the end users.

"The road builders, I think are gonna get killed," he said. "I don’t know how they recover if the AI narrative is correct and AI tools will replace a lot of knowledge workers. Of course, if that’s wrong, then Salesforce is at the greatest sale of a century."

Beyond the companies mentioned by Bowman, a large number of software companies have come under scrutiny in recent weeks, amid what's become known as the "SaaSpocalypse," a recent selloff in Software-as-a-Service and technology stocks driven by fears that artificial intelligence will disrupt traditional software business models.

Here are some other tickers that have been discussed in this framework recently by Seeking Alpha analysts: (SNOW)(DDOG)(SHOP)(HUBS)(FRSH)(WDAY)(INTU)(LZ)(CRWD)(NET)(FTNT)(NOW)(ADSK).

For more Seeking Alpha podcasts, click here.

data.title: Strong Macau traffic during Chinese New Year bodes well for casino revenue
data.description:
Early reports out of Macau on the impact of the Chinese New Year holiday are generally positive. So far, the holiday is seeing record peak‑day visitor traffic but slightly softer cumulative arrivals in comparison to last year. The assessment of analysts is that Macau casino operators are tracking toward posting solid holiday gaming results, perhaps even ahead of expectations.

According to tourism officials, Macau welcomed 551,623 visitors over the first four days of the Chinese New Year holiday, averaging about 138,000 arrivals per day. That total is roughly 8.4% below the same period in 2025, though officials emphasize that daily traffic has consistently exceeded the 100,000‑visitor level widely cited by local businesses as a profitability threshold. Notably, Macau set a new all‑time single‑day record with around 227,943 visitor arrivals and more than 750,000 cross‑boundary movements in total. In terms of casino floor traffic, GGR Asia highlighted moderate levels on day 6 of the 9-day Chinese New Year holiday.

Looking ahead, analysts are generally calling for combined January–February gross gaming revenue to rise roughly 13% year over year after adjusting for the timing of the festival. Growth is expected for both the premium‑mass and mass segments, while the VIP segment is forecast to lag slightly.

Macau-related casino stocks: Wynn Macau (WYNMF) (WYNMY), Wynn Resorts (WYNN), Sands China (SCHYY) (SCHYF), Las Vegas Sands (LVS), MGM China (MCHVF) (MCHVY), MGM Resorts (MGM), Galaxy Entertainment (GXYEF), SJM Holdings (SJMHF) (SJMHY), Melco Resorts & Entertainment (MLCO), and Studio City International (MSC).

data.title: Hamilton signals measured growth with 55% attritional loss ratio guidance amid competitive market
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Hamilton Insurance Group, Ltd. (HG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Giuseppina Albo, CEO, highlighted a period of significant growth since the company's IPO, stating "we've delivered consistently strong results, results that allowed tangible book value per share to grow 67% since the IPO." She emphasized the company's technical underwriting approach and strong client relationships. Albo reported record net income for 2025 of $577 million and a combined ratio of 92.9%, with gross premiums written up 21% to $2.9 billion. She attributed performance to "skilled risk selection, commitment to cycle management and strong broker and client relationships."
  • CEO Albo described a transitioning market environment: "After several hard market years, we now find ourselves in a transitioning market, but importantly, one that still provides ample pockets of attractive opportunities for underwriters like ours who are technical, astute and nimble." She noted that growth going forward "will be more measured than it was in the past."
  • CFO Craig Howie stated, "In 2025, Hamilton had a very strong year of financial results with record net income of $577 million, 44% above the $400 million of net income in 2024." He also highlighted the Bermuda substance-based tax credit as a new benefit, recording a $20.7 million credit in 2025, and announced a special dividend of $2 per common share, with an aggregate payment of approximately $206 million. Howie noted ongoing share repurchases totaling $93 million for the year.

Outlook

  • Management expects the market in 2026 to "remain competitive, but that pricing across the lines of business that we target to remain largely risk adequate." Albo remarked, "while we are confident in our ability to continue to find attractive opportunities, I expect our growth going forward to be more measured than it was in the past."
  • CFO Howie provided guidance for 2026, stating, "we are increasing our catastrophe and headline loss threshold from the current $5 million to $10 million... We would expect the attritional loss ratio to run at about 55% in 2026." He also projected a Bermuda credit of about $27 million for 2026.
  • Corporate expenses are expected to "run between $45 million and $50 million for the year."

Financial Results

  • Hamilton delivered net income of $172 million for Q4 2025, equal to $1.69 per diluted share, with operating income of $168 million, or $1.65 per diluted share. The annualized return on average equity for the quarter was 25%.
  • Underwriting income for the quarter was $76 million, with a group combined ratio of 87.0%. The loss ratio improved to 54.6%, down from 60.1% in the prior period, mainly due to lower net catastrophe losses.
  • Gross premiums written for the quarter increased 23%. Bermuda segment grew 27% in Q4, driven by casualty reinsurance, while International segment premiums increased 20%. Hamilton Select grew 19% during the quarter, focusing on casualty classes.
  • Investment income for Q4 2025 was $98 million. The Two Sigma Hamilton Fund produced a $56 million net return for the quarter; for the year, a net return of 16.0% or $301 million.
  • Shareholders' equity at year-end was $2.8 billion, book value per share was $28.50.

Q&A

  • Hristian Getsov, Wells Fargo: Asked about the underlying loss ratio guide and the decision behind the special dividend. CFO Howie explained the 55% guidance is "the change in our threshold from the $5 million to the $10 million threshold is what's taking that loss pick up to the 55% guidance." On the dividend, Howie said, "A special dividend is an active and an effective way of returning that capital quickly to our shareholders. But we will also be able to continue buying back shares."
  • Getsov also questioned competition in the E&S Select platform. Albo responded that growth was "completely in line with what our expectations were," noting competition is seen on professional lines, not casualty.
  • Thomas Mcjoynt-Griffith, KBW: Asked about premium leverage and data center opportunities. Howie said premium leverage "hasn't really changed very much" and remains at about 80%. Albo said, "we are also a little bit cautious here on accumulation... particularly on the large data centers."
  • Daniel Cohen, BMO: Inquired about reserve developments. Howie stated, "overall, our reserves were favorable for the quarter. None of that came from casualty."
  • Chongwook Lee, Barclays: Asked for returns from the Two Sigma Hamilton fund and about market dynamics in property and casualty. Howie said reporting will now be quarterly with no lag. Albo noted, "On the casualty side, we are still seeing some healthy increases on the insurance side of the equation."
  • David Samar, Citizens: Questioned the source of funds for the special dividend and large losses. Howie stated it is from "available cash on hand as well as the fixed income portfolio," and explained the largest loss was a satellite loss.
  • Matthew Heimermann, Citi: Asked about investing Bermuda tax credit savings and technology. Howie said, "we're going to continue to invest in our projects, our operations and our technology to operate at scale in Bermuda." Albo cited ongoing investment in underwriting and technology, including AI.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on technical questions regarding loss ratio guidance, capital management decisions, and competitive dynamics. Questions sought clarity on guidance changes and the company’s strategic positioning.
  • Management's sentiment was confident and disciplined in prepared remarks, with Albo stating, "we are confident in our ability to continue to find attractive opportunities." During Q&A, responses remained composed and explanatory, reflecting a commitment to transparency and measured growth. Compared to the previous quarter, the tone was slightly more measured regarding future growth, but confidence in technical execution and capital discipline persisted.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted to emphasize more measured growth in 2026, with explicit commentary on maintaining discipline and not chasing top-line growth at the expense of margins.
  • The attritional loss ratio guidance for 2026 was raised to 55%, influenced by a change in the catastrophe and headline loss threshold, compared to the prior quarter's focus around 54.4%.
  • Management continued to highlight strong capital position, but introduced a special dividend and updated expectations for the Bermuda tax credit.
  • Analysts continued to focus on underwriting results, capital management, and competitive positioning, mirroring themes from the previous quarter but probing new guidance and changes in business mix.
  • The tone from management remained confident, but with greater emphasis on market competition and the need for disciplined, selective growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged a transitioning market with increasing competition, particularly in property and professional lines, and indicated selective underwriting as a mitigation strategy.
  • Elevated large losses were noted for the quarter, with CFO Howie citing a satellite loss as the largest event, prompting changes in loss threshold reporting.
  • The company remains cautious on emerging opportunities such as data centers, monitoring accumulation risk and avoiding extensive exposure to business interruption claims.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the sustainability of reserve favorability, capital deployment, and the impact of market competition on future growth and margins.

Final Takeaway

Hamilton Insurance Group closed 2025 with record financial results, strong underwriting and investment performance, and a robust capital position. Looking ahead, management signaled a disciplined approach to growth in a more competitive market, guiding to a 55% attritional loss ratio and prioritizing sustainable profitability over top-line expansion. The company’s capital management strategy includes both a special dividend and ongoing share repurchases, reflecting confidence in its balance sheet and operational resilience while remaining vigilant to market risks and selective in new business opportunities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Top Oil And Gas Refining and Marketing Stocks by Year-To-Date Performance
data.description:
Year to date, the energy sector has been one of the market’s strongest performers. In light of this, below is a list of the top oil and gas refining and marketing stocks ranked by their Year-To-Date performance. 

The list is topped by Calumet (CLMT), with a YTD performance of 47.91%. Green Plains (GPRE) and Clean Energy Fuels (CLNE) follow as the next top contenders in the sector.

Other major players rounding out the top five include PBF Energy (PBF) and Valero Energy (VLO). The list also features established names like Marathon Petroleum (MPC) and Phillips 66 (PSX), along with Par Pacific (PARR) and Sunoco LP (SUN)

Here is the list:

Energy ETFs: (XLE), (AMLP), (VDE), (XOP), (OIH), and (IXC)

data.title: BorgWarner's new venture with TurboCell poses risks not yet priced in -- UBS
data.description:
BorgWarner (BWA) is offsetting deceleration in its core business by branching out into data center power generation, an industry poised for explosive growth, and a decision that should translate into meaningful gains in the company’s top-line.

However, BorgWarner (BWA) has yet to provide concrete details on its new opportunity with TurboCell. With much of the potential already reflected in the share price, UBS has adopted a more cautious stance, downgrading the stock to Sell from Neutral.

“To be clear, we applaud BWA for seeking new growth opportunities,” analyst Joseph Spak said in his note to clients. “But we believe the bull case assumes fully scaled $1.5B in sales by 2030…we expect a slower ramp to half that level,” Spak adds, assuming that if even the bull case is correct, 50% of the upside has already been priced in.

Rather than simply moving to the sidelines, Spak believes BorgWarner (BWA) is a sell at current levels, not because of the risks of the power generation business, but instead because of risks specific to the BorgWarner (BWA) and TurboCell partnership.

First, TurboCell is a startup, and BWA has never made turbines at scale. This combo “amplifies the execution risk,” Spak says.

Second, BWA indicated the cost of ownership is compelling. But without pricing or detailed specifications, Spak thinks this is difficult to verify.

And finally, the product is still in development and not yet commercialized. While there is a “built-in” buyer with Edged through TurboCell’s parent Endeavor, Spak is uneasy with the lack of public information on either Endeavor, Edged, or TurboCell.

“Until the product is more proven, bringing on third-party customers could take time,” he says.

With these considerations, as well as underwhelming expectations for BWA’s core automotive business, “the scenarios that investors have to believe to justify upside may prove to be quite optimistic.”

Spak’s Sell rating on BorgWarner (BWA) is a notable outlier among not only Wall Street analysts (Deutsche Bank recently upgraded the stock to a Buy) and Seeking Alpha analysts—all of whom view BorgWarner (BWA) as a Buy—but also Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating, which views BorgWarner (BWA) as a Strong Buy with a Quant score of 4.54 out of 5.

After setting an all-time high last week on Q4 results/TurboCell news, BorgWarner (BWA) shares have retreated, spending the subsequent five consecutive days in the red.
